reasoning: The 1-year Treasury bill yield has been trending downward, consistent with market expectations of Fed rate cuts through 2026 to around 2.9%. The most recent inflation data (CPI released on Friday, Feb 13, 2026) came in cooler than expected at 2.4%, which caused Treasury yields to fall that day (the 1-year T-bill dropped to 3.30%). Because short-term interest rates exhibit negative drift in an environment of expected rate cuts (an inverted forward curve), the baseline expectation is for the yield to systematically decrease or stay flat over time. Short-term downside momentum and macroeconomic expectations of further Fed cuts suggest values are more likely to fall than rise from the current ~3.30 baseline. In line with the instructions, uncertainty is assumed to increase at the longer horizon due to vulnerability to broad macro shocks (e.g. potential for warmer PCE data or hawkish Fed rhetoric leading up to the March FOMC meeting), bringing the March 17 probability closer to 50%.
